questions.to_find: What is printed when the program executes, given arrays of ints and an array of pointers?

understand.plain: The program defines three integer arrays and an array x of pointers to those arrays. The print function receives this array of pointers and uses different pointer/array indexing expressions to print four values. We must trace each printf to determine the output.

keywords.explain: A variable like int *x[] holds pointers; x[0] is a pointer to the first element of a1, x[1] to a2, x[2] to a3.
*(a[1]+3) means start at the pointer a[1] (which is a2), advance 3 elements, then dereference — same as a2[3].
The unary * operator accesses the value at the memory address held by a pointer.

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' width='540' height='200'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect class='bg' x='5' y='5' width='530' height='190' rx='8'/><text class='lbl ink' x='20' y='30'>x[] pointer array</text><rect class='hi' x='20' y='40' width='80' height='30' rx='4'/><text class='sub ink' x='30' y='60'>x[0]=a1</text><rect class='bg' x='20' y='80' width='80' height='30' rx='4'/><text class='sub ink' x='30' y='100'>x[1]=a2</text><rect class='bg' x='20' y='120' width='80' height='30' rx='4'/><text class='sub ink' x='30' y='140'>x[2]=a3</text><text class='lbl ink' x='160' y='30'>a1: {6,7,8,18,34,67}</text><rect class='hi' x='200' y='40' width='40' height='30' rx='4'/><text class='sub ink' x='208' y='60'>a1[2]=8</text><text class='lbl ink' x='160' y='100'>a2: {23,56,28,29}</text><rect class='hi' x='280' y='110' width='40' height='30' rx='4'/><text class='sub ink' x='284' y='130'>a2[3]=29</text><text class='lbl ink' x='160' y='155'>a3: {-12,27,-31}</text><rect class='hi' x='160' y='163' width='50' height='25' rx='4'/><text class='sub ink' x='163' y='180'>a3[0]=-12</text><rect class='hi' x='215' y='163' width='40' height='25' rx='4'/><text class='sub ink' x='218' y='180'>a3[1]=27</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Diagram showing x as an array of 3 pointers. x[0] points to a1 (highlighting index 2 = 8), x[1] points to a2 (highlighting index 3 = 29), x[2] points to a3 (highlighting index 0 = -12 and index 1 = 27).

given.aim: Trace four printf calls using pointer array indexing to determine the printed output.

terms.meaning: a is the parameter equal to x; a[0]=a1, so a[0][2]=a1[2]=8
a[1]=a2, advance pointer by 3, dereference = a2[3]=29

given.plan: Substitute a = x in the print function. Then evaluate each expression using the definitions of a1, a2, a3 and standard C pointer/array equivalences.

steps.title: Establish the binding: a = x
steps.apply: When main calls print(x), the parameter a inside print holds the same pointer array x. So a[0]=a1, a[1]=a2, a[2]=a3.
steps.note: In C, array names decay to pointers; passing x passes the address of x[0].

steps.title: Evaluate each printf
steps.note: All four values match option A. The other options either start with negative numbers or have wrong values.

questions.formula_note: C array-of-pointers indexing: a[i][j] is equivalent to *(*(a+i)+j). Pointer arithmetic: *(p+k) == p[k].

questions.to_find: What value does the program print for the given array {2, -2, -1, 3, 4, 2}?

understand.plain: The program attempts to find the maximum sum of a non-decreasing subarray (segment where each element is not less than the previous). It resets the running sum whenever it encounters a negative number or a value smaller than the previous element. After the loop it does a final check and prints the maximum sum found.

keywords.explain: Accumulates the sum of the current non-decreasing positive segment. Resets when the segment breaks.
Tracks the highest running sum seen so far across all segments.
keywords.example: Once sum reaches 7 and then breaks, maxsum becomes 7.

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' width='500' height='180'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect class='bg' x='5' y='5' width='490' height='170' rx='8'/><text class='lbl ink' x='20' y='30'>Array: a[] = {2, -2, -1, 3, 4, 2}</text><text class='sub mut' x='20' y='55'>Index:    0    1    2   3   4   5</text><rect class='hi' x='20' y='65' width='55' height='28' rx='4'/><text class='sub ink' x='28' y='83'>i=0: sum=2</text><rect class='no' x='85' y='65' width='60' height='28' rx='4'/><text class='sub ink' x='90' y='83'>i=1: reset, sum=0</text><rect class='no' x='155' y='65' width='60' height='28' rx='4'/><text class='sub ink' x='160' y='83'>i=2: reset, sum=0</text><rect class='hi' x='225' y='65' width='55' height='28' rx='4'/><text class='sub ink' x='230' y='83'>i=3: sum=3</text><rect class='hi' x='290' y='65' width='55' height='28' rx='4'/><text class='sub ink' x='295' y='83'>i=4: sum=7</text><rect class='bg' x='355' y='65' width='60' height='28' rx='4'/><text class='sub ink' x='360' y='83'>i=5: reset</text><text class='lbl ink' x='20' y='130'>maxsum updated: 2 -> 2 (at i=1), then 2 -> 7 (at i=5)</text><text class='big ink' x='20' y='165'>Final maxsum = 7</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Timeline of the loop iterations showing sum and maxsum evolving. At i=1 (value -2) sum resets and maxsum becomes 2. At i=3,4 (values 3,4) sum grows to 7. At i=5 (value 2 < 4) reset triggers and maxsum becomes 7. After loop sum=2 is not > 7.

given.aim: Trace the loop iteration by iteration and find the final maxsum value.

terms.meaning: i==0 OR a[i]<0 OR a[i]<a[i-1] triggers a segment break and resets sum
terms.example: At i=5, a[5]=2 < a[4]=4, so reset fires.
terms.connects: When reset fires, maxsum is updated if sum > maxsum, then sum is restarted.

terms.meaning: sum = (a[i]>0) ? a[i] : 0; starts a new segment from the current element if positive, else 0
terms.example: At i=1, a[1]=-2 not >0, so sum=0.
terms.connects: Negative elements never start a new segment with positive value.

terms.meaning: After the loop, if sum > maxsum then maxsum = sum; handles the last segment
terms.example: At end, sum=2, maxsum=7; 2 not > 7, no update.
terms.connects: Ensures the final ongoing segment is also considered.

given.plan: Trace each iteration: determine whether the reset condition fires; if yes update maxsum and restart sum; if no add to sum. After the loop do the final check. Print maxsum.

steps.title: Trace iterations i=0 through i=5
steps.formula_raw: if (i==0 || a[i]<0 || a[i]<a[i-1]) { if(sum>maxsum) maxsum=sum; sum = a[i]>0 ? a[i]:0; } else sum+=a[i];
steps.apply:
i=0: reset (i==0). maxsum=0, sum=0; sum=2>0 => sum=2.
i=1: a[1]=-2<0, reset. sum=2>maxsum=0 => maxsum=2. sum=-2 not>0 => sum=0.
i=2: a[2]=-1<0, reset. sum=0 not>maxsum=2. sum=-1 not>0 => sum=0.
i=3: a[3]=3, not <0, 3 not < a[2]=-1, no reset. else: sum+=3 => sum=3.
i=4: a[4]=4, not <0, 4 not < a[3]=3, no reset. else: sum+=4 => sum=7.
i=5: a[5]=2 < a[4]=4, reset. sum=7>maxsum=2 => maxsum=7. sum=2>0 => sum=2.
steps.note: The key segment giving the maximum is {3,4} summing to 7.

steps.title: Post-loop check and print
steps.apply: After loop: sum=2, maxsum=7. 2 is not > 7, so maxsum stays 7. printf prints 7.

questions.formula_note: Loop simulation / dry-run technique: trace variables iteration by iteration to determine program output. The algorithm computes the maximum sum of any non-decreasing positive-starting contiguous subarray.

questions.question:
The goal of structured programming is to:

A. have well indented programs
B. be able to infer the flow of control from the compiled code
C. be able to infer the flow of control from the program text
D. avoid the use of GOTO statements
questions.answer: C. be able to infer the flow of control from the program text
questions.to_find: What is the primary goal of structured programming?

understand.plain: Structured programming is a programming paradigm that emerged in the 1960s and 1970s. Its central idea is that programs should be written using only three control structures (sequence, selection, iteration), so that a reader can understand the flow of execution simply by reading the source code — without tracing runtime state or compiled output.

keywords.explain: A programming style using only sequential, conditional (if/else), and loop (while/for) constructs, avoiding arbitrary jumps like GOTO.
keywords.example: A program with only if-else and while loops is structured.

keywords.explain: The order in which individual statements, instructions, or function calls are executed at runtime.
keywords.example: In an if-else, control flows to the true-branch or false-branch based on the condition.

understand.visual_alt: The concept of structured programming: source code text alone is sufficient to determine the execution path. Three allowed control structures: sequence (top-to-bottom), selection (if-else), and iteration (loops). GOTO is excluded but avoiding GOTO is a means, not the goal itself.

given.aim: Identify the fundamental goal of the structured programming paradigm.

terms.meaning: Edsger Dijkstra argued programs should have a clear correspondence between static text and dynamic execution.
terms.example: His 1968 letter 'Go To Statement Considered Harmful' laid the foundation.
terms.connects: The goal is readability and verifiability of control flow from source text.

terms.meaning: Indentation is a formatting practice, not the goal of structured programming.
terms.example: A poorly indented program can still be structured.
terms.connects: This is a superficial property; incorrect answer.

terms.meaning: Avoiding GOTO is a consequence/means of structured programming, not its primary goal.
terms.example: Structured programs happen to not need GOTO, but the goal is control-flow readability.
terms.connects: The question asks for the goal, not the method.

given.plan: Recall the definition of structured programming from software engineering theory. The primary goal is that control flow should be deducible from reading the program text (source code), not the compiled binary or runtime trace.

steps.title: Eliminate incorrect options
steps.apply:
A (indentation) is a style concern, not the paradigm's goal.
B (infer flow from compiled code) is about reverse engineering binaries — not structured programming.
D (avoid GOTO) is a consequence/tool, not the stated goal.
steps.note: The motivation behind structured programming was always about making code understandable to humans reading the source.

steps.title: Confirm option C
steps.apply: Structured programming's stated goal (Dijkstra, Hoare, Wirth) is that a programmer should be able to understand the execution flow from the program text alone, without running it. This is precisely option C.
steps.note: Avoiding GOTO is the mechanism; inferring control flow from program text is the goal.

solution.result: C. be able to infer the flow of control from the program text

questions.formula_note: Structured programming paradigm (Dijkstra, 1968): programs written with sequence, selection, and iteration constructs so that control flow is statically apparent from the source code text.
